story:
Miriam rose before the sun, every day. Her body woke her at 4:47 a.m.—the minute Peter used to stir beside her before walking out to the shed to listen to the radio. Thirty years since his passing, and still her eyes opened on that ghostly beat. She had thought grief would dull; it didn’t. It only learned to sit still, waiting.

In the half-dark of her kitchen, she moved by muscle memory: switch on the single lamp; fill the copper kettle; light the gas; wait for the whistle. She chose tea as a purpose, the way her daughter Elaine had urged after the funeral—something small she could count on. The cup had been her grandmother’s, white porcelain, the blue ring worn faint by decades of lips. Every morning she added one teaspoon of honey and two teaspoons of loose tea—never three—stirred counterclockwise, and sat in the creaking chair by the window overlooking the lilac bush. At precisely 5:03 she raised the cup and whispered, “Here’s to another day.” No one knew she said the words aloud. They were private, like prayer.

For years, the ritual stayed steady while the world changed around her. It was enough. Until the morning the steam shifted.

Vapor curled in deliberate shapes. Once, she saw a face—Peter’s—half-smile, half-sorrow, gone before she blinked. She told herself it was fatigue. But when she whispered, “Here’s to another day,” a voice—clear, quiet, right at her ear—answered, “And another still.” The cup trembled. Tea burned her wrist. The next morning brought, “And another night.” It was Peter’s voice, rough and gentle, the way he sounded reading to her in bed.

Silence the following day stung worse than fear. She began to chase the sound: new teas, different honey, a swapped kettle; the cup moved to different windowsills as if angle mattered. Sometimes she brewed three, four cups before dawn. She started asking questions—Are you there? Do you hear me?—then bolder ones: Is it you? Peter? When the whisper returned, it carried warmth and sorrow. “I’ve missed you.” Shapes bloomed in steam—heart, wave, hand—and answers seemed to pulse through the heat. “Are you waiting for me?” The steam rose, then curled—a nod. A terrible, beautiful hope took root.

Elaine called, worried. Miriam assured her she was fine. She didn’t say she’d stopped church, bridge, and the walks to the post office; that nights were for dreams of the fog-thick lake where they’d honeymooned, Peter waist-deep, hand outstretched. Mornings brought questions from him too. “Do you still keep my watch?” “By the bed.” “Do you still think of the lake?” “Every summer.” “Do you want to see me again?” “More than anything.” The tea’s surface shimmered like a heated mirror; beyond it, a shape she could not name. The ritual no longer felt like remembering. It felt like returning.

On the thirtieth dawn she broke her rule and added a third spoonful of tea. Impossibly, the kettle screamed before she lit the burner. Windows fogged. “Here’s to another day,” she whispered. “And another forever,” the voice replied. Lights flickered; the walls breathed heat. The cup seemed to deepen, its surface falling away. Peter waited there—young, blue-eyed, kind—reaching. She touched him. Warmth flooded her. A soft tug. The kitchen dissolved into white.

That afternoon the mailman found her door ajar. The house was tidy, the kettle cool. Only the cup remained, a thin curl of steam still rising. They never found Miriam.

Two days later Elaine arrived to pack up. The air smelled of honey and smoke. The lilacs were in improbable bloom. She lifted the cup and saw tiny ripples cross the cold tea. Her mother’s voice came, soft and steady: “Here’s to another day.” A second voice answered, “And another still.” The cup slipped and shattered, the sound fading like a heartbeat. Outside, the lilacs swayed though there was no wind.
